title: Freshly initialized markets are immediately borrowable at a zero exchange rate
claim: `init()` sets the collateral/oracle fields but never seeds or validates `exchangeRate`. Until `updateExchangeRate()` succeeds at least once, the cached rate stays at its zero default, and every solvency check in `borrow()`, `removeCollateral()` and `cook()` trusts that zero value. With `_exchangeRate == 0`, `_isSolvent()` reduces the debt side of the comparison to zero, so any borrower with nonzero collateral is treated as solvent regardless of debt size.
impact: The first borrower in a newly created market can post dust collateral and drain all MIM available in the Cauldron before anyone performs a successful oracle update. If the configured oracle keeps returning `updated == false` or otherwise never seeds a nonzero cached rate, the market can remain permanently unliquidatable while bad debt accumulates.
paths: Deploy or clone a new Cauldron -> fund it with MIM -> attacker adds minimal collateral -> attacker calls `borrow()` before any successful `updateExchangeRate()` -> solvency check passes at `exchangeRate == 0` -> attacker drains available MIM

Deploy a market with an oracle that never returns an updated rate -> cached `exchangeRate` remains zero -> attacker repeatedly borrows against tiny collateral and cannot be liquidated using the same zero-rate solvency logic

title: Solvency-critical actions trust arbitrarily stale cached oracle prices
claim: The protocol stores a single cached `exchangeRate` and never enforces freshness for solvency-sensitive actions. `borrow()` and `removeCollateral()` only call `accrue()` before checking solvency, and `cook()` reuses the cached storage value unless the caller explicitly inserts `ACTION_UPDATE_EXCHANGE_RATE`. Even liquidation accepts stale data by calling `updateExchangeRate()` and falling back to the previous cached rate whenever `oracle.get()` returns `updated == false`.
impact: After adverse price moves, borrowers can race keepers and borrow too much or withdraw too much collateral against a stale favorable rate, leaving the market undercollateralized once a fresh price arrives. In the opposite direction, if the cached rate is stale-high and the oracle update fails, healthy users can be liquidated against an obsolete punitive price.
paths: Collateral price falls -> cached rate is still low -> borrower calls `borrow()` or `removeCollateral()` without refreshing the oracle -> stale solvency check passes -> protocol inherits bad debt when the exchange rate is eventually refreshed
Collateral price recovers -> cached rate remains high -> `oracle.get()` fails during `liquidate()` so the function reuses the stale cached rate -> healthy users are liquidated at an outdated price

title: `cook()` max-rate protection is inverted and only passes above the caller's ceiling
claim: `ACTION_UPDATE_EXCHANGE_RATE` decodes `(must_update, minRate, maxRate)` but enforces `(maxRate == 0 || rate > maxRate)` instead of the intended upper-bound check `rate <= maxRate`. As written, any nonzero `maxRate` makes the action revert for acceptable prices and succeed only once the oracle price is already worse than the caller's declared ceiling.
impact: Users and integrations cannot rely on `cook()` for oracle slippage protection. Atomic sequences that are supposed to abort when the oracle exceeds a tolerated bound can instead execute only at explicitly disallowed prices, undermining protected borrow and collateral-withdraw workflows.
paths: Integrator builds a `cook()` bundle with `ACTION_UPDATE_EXCHANGE_RATE(maxRate = X)` before `ACTION_BORROW` or `ACTION_REMOVE_COLLATERAL`
Oracle rate moves above `X` -> the inverted predicate still passes -> the protected action executes under a price the caller explicitly tried to forbid

title: Permissionless `cook()` calls can sweep stray ETH and directly transferred tokens from the Cauldron
claim: The Cauldron accepts ETH in payable entry points (`init()` and `cook()`), and `cook()` forwards each `values[i]` into `_bentoDeposit()` or `_call()` from the contract's own ETH balance without ever enforcing that the total forwarded ETH is bounded by the caller's `msg.value`. Any ETH already sitting on the Cauldron can therefore be spent by a later caller. Separately, `ACTION_CALL` lets anyone execute arbitrary external calls from the Cauldron context, so ERC20 tokens that were mistakenly transferred directly to the Cauldron can be swept with a token `transfer()` call.
impact: Any ETH stranded on the Cauldron—such as deployment ETH sent to payable `init()`, overpaid `cook()` value, or force-sent ETH—can be stolen by the next arbitrary caller. Tokens accidentally sent directly to the Cauldron instead of BentoBox can likewise be permissionlessly transferred out to an attacker.
paths: ETH is left on the Cauldron via payable `init()`, an overfunded `cook()` call, or forced transfer -> attacker calls `cook()` with `ACTION_CALL` (or another value-consuming action) and a nonzero `values[i]` -> the contract spends its preexisting ETH balance for the attacker

A victim transfers an ERC20 directly to the Cauldron address -> attacker calls `cook()` with `ACTION_CALL` targeting that token's `transfer(attacker, amount)` -> the token balance held by the Cauldron is swept
